impacts.legalQuestion: Whether a patent holder's conditioning of patent licenses on exclusive purchases of unpatented products constitutes unlawful tying under the Sherman Act, and whether the defense that tied products are 'nonstaple goods' lacking substantial non-infringing uses shields the patent holder from antitrust liability.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed a jury verdict finding that Ingevity engaged in unlawful tying by conditioning licenses to its fuel vapor canister patent on customers' exclusive purchase of Ingevity's unpatented carbon honeycomb products. The court upheld the antitrust damages award, rejecting Ingevity's argument that its honeycombs were nonstaple goods protected from antitrust liability under the patent laws. The district court had found that whether the honeycombs had substantial non-infringing uses was a jury question.
impacts.positiveImplications: Businesses accused of anticompetitive tying by patent holders may use this decision to support claims that patent licensing conditioned on exclusive product purchases violates antitrust law, expanding available remedies against such practices.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders who bundle product purchase requirements with patent licenses face significant antitrust exposure; the nonstaple goods defense may be narrowly construed, leaving companies vulnerable to tying claims even when their products are related to patented technology.

impacts.caseName: The Trustees of Columbia University v. Gen Digital Inc.
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether patent claims directed to detecting anomalous program executions by comparing function calls made in an emulator to a model of expected function calls are abstract and ineligible for patenting under 35 U.S.C. § 101.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit vacated a $185 million infringement judgment, holding that Columbia's computer security patents claiming methods for detecting anomalous program executions were abstract at step one of the Alice analysis. The court also held the district court erred in allowing damages based on Norton's foreign sales and reversed a contempt order against Norton's counsel Quinn Emanuel. The case was remanded for further proceedings on Alice step two.
impacts.positiveImplications: Technology companies defending against software patent assertions gain a significant precedent limiting the scope of patentable subject matter for computer security detection methods, potentially invalidating similar patents and reducing litigation exposure.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders with claims directed to software-based detection and comparison methods face heightened invalidity risk under § 101; the foreign sales damages ruling also limits potential recovery in cross-border infringement cases.

impacts.caseName: GoTV Streaming, LLC v. Netflix, Inc.
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether patents claiming server-based methods of tailoring content delivery specifications to match a wireless device's screen size and capabilities are ineligible for patenting under 35 U.S.C. § 101.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit held on cross-appeal that GoTV's streaming media patents were ineligible under § 101, reversing a $2.5 million jury verdict in GoTV's favor on the '715 patent. The court found the claims directed to the abstract idea of tailoring content specifications to device capabilities. The court also expressly vacated the district court's rulings dismissing induced infringement and admitting certain damages evidence, though those issues became moot given the ineligibility holding.
impacts.positiveImplications: Streaming and content delivery companies benefit from a strong precedent that basic content-tailoring methods for different devices are abstract ideas ineligible for patent protection, reducing patent troll exposure in the streaming technology space.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders with claims covering adaptive content delivery or device-specific rendering face increased invalidity risk; the decision further narrows the scope of patentable software innovations in the media streaming industry.

impacts.caseName: Ascendis Pharma A/S v. BioMarin Pharmaceutical Inc.
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether an ITC respondent may obtain a mandatory stay under 28 U.S.C. § 1659(a)(2) of a refiled declaratory judgment action when it had previously missed the deadline to seek a stay of the original declaratory judgment action involving the same parties and issues.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit held that BioMarin could not circumvent the statutory deadline for seeking a mandatory stay under § 1659(a)(2) by voluntarily dismissing and refiling its declaratory judgment action after the 30-day window had expired. The court found this tactic impermissible, preserving Ascendis's right to proceed with its district court declaratory judgment action alongside the pending ITC investigation.
impacts.positiveImplications: Companies facing parallel ITC and district court patent proceedings benefit from certainty that opponents cannot manipulate § 1659(a) stay deadlines through strategic dismissal and refiling, preserving access to district court adjudication.
impacts.negativeImplications: ITC respondents who miss the 30-day mandatory stay window lose the ability to delay parallel district court proceedings, potentially forcing them to litigate on two fronts simultaneously with increased costs.

impacts.caseName: Global Tubing LLC v. Tenaris Coiled Tubes LLC
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether genuine disputes of material fact precluded summary judgment on inequitable conduct in patent prosecution and on a Walker Process fraud claim for attempted monopolization based on the same conduct.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit vacated summary judgments that had been granted to both parties—finding that fact disputes precluded summary judgment both on Global Tubing's inequitable conduct defense (granted against Tenaris) and on Global Tubing's Walker Process antitrust claim (granted in favor of Tenaris). The case involved allegations that Tenaris failed to disclose prior art documents (the CYMAX Documents) during prosecution of its coiled tubing patents, potentially constituting fraud on the patent office.
impacts.positiveImplications: Competitors facing potentially fraudulently-obtained patents have a pathway to pursue both inequitable conduct defenses and Walker Process antitrust claims; the decision ensures these fact-intensive issues reach trial rather than being resolved on summary judgment.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders face extended and costly litigation when prior art disclosure issues are raised, as the court set a high bar for obtaining summary judgment on inequitable conduct defenses, creating uncertainty about patent enforceability until trial.

impacts.caseName: Apple Inc. v. International Trade Commission
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the ITC correctly found that Apple's importation and sale of Apple Watch models incorporating blood oxygen measurement functionality violated § 337 of the Tariff Act by infringing Masimo's wearable pulse oximetry patents.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed the ITC's finding that Apple violated § 337 by importing Apple Watch models that infringed Masimo's patents on wearable blood oxygen measurement devices. The court upheld the Commission's domestic industry determination, validity rulings, and infringement findings, sustaining a limited exclusion order barring importation of the infringing Apple Watch models.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent holders asserting ITC complaints against major technology importers receive strong precedent affirming the Commission's authority to issue exclusion orders on consumer electronics products, providing a powerful enforcement mechanism.
impacts.negativeImplications: Technology companies incorporating patented sensor technology into consumer products face significant supply chain disruption risk from ITC exclusion orders; this decision confirms the viability of using § 337 to block importation of high-volume consumer electronics.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the district court erred in excluding a patent plaintiff's damages expert report and granting summary judgment for absence of a remedy based on that exclusion.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed the district court's exclusion of Exafer's damages expert report in a patent infringement case against Microsoft involving Azure cloud networking technology. The court also vacated the grant of summary judgment that had been based solely on the absence of a damages remedy following the expert's exclusion, and vacated the denial of Exafer's motion to reopen discovery.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent plaintiffs gain protection against overly aggressive Daubert exclusions of damages experts, as appellate courts will scrutinize whether exclusion was warranted before allowing it to serve as the basis for summary judgment on remedy grounds.
impacts.negativeImplications: Defendants who successfully exclude damages experts at the district court level face the risk of reversal on appeal, prolonging costly patent litigation and reopening discovery.

impacts.caseName: 112 Genesee Street, LLC v. United States
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the Restaurant Revitalization Fund statute created a mandatory obligation for the SBA to award grants to eligible applicants in the order applications were received, giving rise to Tucker Act jurisdiction for claims by applicants who were skipped.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed that the Court of Federal Claims had Tucker Act jurisdiction over claims by over 300 restaurants that applied for RRF grants on the first day applications opened but were skipped in favor of later applicants. The court held the statute's directive that the SBA 'shall award grants' in the order applications were received was mandatory, creating money-mandating obligations enforceable by applicants who were improperly denied grants before the fund was exhausted.
impacts.positiveImplications: Businesses that were eligible but denied federal grant funding due to agency processing errors have a viable damages claim under the Tucker Act, establishing that mandatory statutory grant language creates enforceable rights.
impacts.negativeImplications: The government faces potential liability for hundreds of millions in unpaid grant obligations when agencies fail to follow statutory priority rules in distributing program funds, even after appropriations have been rescinded.

impacts.caseName: National Veterans Legal Services Program v. United States
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the district court properly approved a $125 million class action settlement of claims that the federal judiciary charged excessive PACER fees beyond those authorized by statute.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed approval of a $125 million nationwide class action settlement resolving claims that the judiciary used PACER fees to fund projects unrelated to PACER operations, in violation of the E-Government Act's requirement that fees be charged 'only to the extent necessary.' The court upheld the settlement terms, attorneys' fees, and incentive awards to class representatives, rejecting an objector's challenges on jurisdictional, substantive, and procedural grounds.
impacts.positiveImplications: Businesses and law firms that are heavy PACER users will receive substantial fee refunds; the settlement validates that statutory fee limitations on government electronic records access are enforceable through class action litigation.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the ITC's practice of automatically designating all questionnaire responses in antidumping and countervailing duty investigations as confidential is consistent with the governing statute, and whether the common law right of public access to judicial records applies to such information.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed the Court of International Trade's holding that the ITC's blanket practice of automatically designating all questionnaire responses as confidential is incompatible with the statute, and that 19 U.S.C. § 1516a does not abrogate the common law right of access to judicial records. The court treated the ITC's mandamus petition as a notice of appeal under the collateral order doctrine.
impacts.positiveImplications: Businesses involved in trade remedy proceedings benefit from greater transparency, as competitors and the public may gain access to previously sealed questionnaire responses, improving the ability to monitor and challenge trade duty determinations.
impacts.negativeImplications: Companies that submit commercially sensitive information in ITC questionnaires during antidumping and countervailing duty investigations face increased risk of disclosure; businesses must now affirmatively seek and justify confidential treatment rather than relying on automatic blanket protection.

impacts.caseName: US Patent No. 7,679,637 LLC v. Google LLC
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether patent claims directed to web conferencing systems with time-shifting capabilities enabling participants to observe sessions in real-time, delayed, or after completion are eligible for patenting under 35 U.S.C. § 101.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed dismissal of a patent infringement suit against Google, holding that the asserted claims of a web conferencing patent were ineligible under § 101. The patent claimed systems allowing participants to asynchronously view multimedia presentation data streams at different playback rates while maintaining audio quality. The court found the claims failed to state a claim upon which relief could be granted.
impacts.positiveImplications: Technology companies operating web conferencing and streaming platforms benefit from another precedent finding that time-shifting and asynchronous viewing features are abstract ideas ineligible for patent protection, reducing patent assertion risk.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders with claims covering web conferencing time-shifting and playback features face increased invalidity risk, further limiting the enforceability of software method patents in the communications technology space.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the PTO Director's instructions to the PTAB governing discretionary denials of inter partes review institution constitute a 'general statement of policy' exempt from APA notice-and-comment rulemaking requirements under 5 U.S.C. § 553(b).
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed that the PTO Director's instructions to the Board on exercising discretion to deny IPR institution are general statements of policy exempt from notice-and-comment rulemaking. The court emphasized that Congress provided no legal right to IPR institution, the Director retains ultimate non-institution authority insulated from judicial review, and the instructions do not purport to bind the Director.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent holders benefit from the preservation of the PTO Director's broad discretion to deny IPR institution through internal policy guidance without formal rulemaking, reducing the ability of challengers to force more IPR proceedings.
impacts.negativeImplications: Companies seeking to use IPR to challenge patents face continued uncertainty, as the PTO Director retains broad, judicially unreviewable discretion to deny institution through informal policy directives that need not undergo public notice and comment.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the PTAB correctly construed the term 'encryption information' in a streaming media patent to sustain patentability over prior art in an inter partes review challenging the patent as obvious.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed the Board's claim construction of 'encryption information' in DivX's streaming media encryption patent, vacated the Board's decision sustaining the patent, and remanded for further proceedings. The court held the Board majority's construction was inconsistent with the claim language and specification, which required that encryption information identify encrypted portions of video frames.
impacts.positiveImplications: Streaming and media companies challenging encryption-related patents in IPR proceedings benefit from the Federal Circuit's willingness to correct Board claim construction errors that improperly shield patents from invalidity challenges.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders whose claims survived IPR face continued vulnerability on appeal if the Board's claim construction is not well-supported by the intrinsic record.

impacts.caseName: Willis Electric Co., Ltd. v. Polygroup Ltd. (Macao Commercial Offshore)
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the district court correctly denied JMOL of obviousness and a new trial on damages for a patent covering pre-lit artificial tree trunk connectors that establish simultaneous mechanical and electrical connections.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed denial of Polygroup's motion for JMOL of obviousness and new trial on damages regarding Willis's patent on pre-lit artificial trees with integrated trunk connectors. The court upheld the jury's finding that the prior art did not render the claimed coaxial trunk connector design obvious, sustaining Willis's patent and the associated damages award in a long-running dispute over holiday tree technology.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent holders in consumer product markets receive confirmation that non-obvious mechanical design innovations are protectable, supporting enforcement against competitors who copy integrated product features.
impacts.negativeImplications: Manufacturers of competing consumer products face sustained infringement liability when they cannot establish obviousness of mechanical design patents, even in seemingly simple product categories.

impacts.caseName: Fortress Iron, LP v. Digger Specialties, Inc.
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether patents are invalid for omission of a coinventor when the patent holder acknowledges the omission but cannot locate the missing coinventor to obtain consent for correction under 35 U.S.C. § 256.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed that Fortress's railing patents were invalid for omitting a Chinese coinventor (Huang) whom Fortress could not locate to add under § 256. The court also affirmed the denial of Fortress's motion to judicially correct inventorship under § 256(b), holding that the inability to locate a known coinventor does not excuse the inventorship defect. This creates a practical trap where acknowledged inventorship errors can render patents unenforceable.
impacts.positiveImplications: Accused infringers can defend against patent assertions by identifying omitted coinventors, particularly in cases involving international collaborators who may be difficult to locate, providing a powerful invalidity defense.
impacts.negativeImplications: Companies that collaborate with foreign manufacturers or contractors on product development face significant patent validity risk if all contributors to the invention are not properly identified as coinventors at the outset, as later correction may be impossible if collaborators cannot be located.
